titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Josiah Rogers Papers
titlestmt.author: compiled by Staff of Manuscripts and Archives

conventiondeclaration.abbr: Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual
conventiondeclaration.citation: translation missing: en.enumerations.resource_finding_aid_description_rules.Finding aid created in accordance with Manuscripts and Archives Processing Manual

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday February 12, 2020 at 08:25

did.abstract: Correspondence and legal papers of Josiah Rogers who was a blacksmith, miller and farmer in Branford. The papers document his extensive land transactions, largely in Branford, but also involving property in Milford, Stratford, New London, Killingworth, New Haven and Waterbury. Signatories to the documents include Joseph Talcott, Gurdon Saltonstall, John Russell and Edward Barker.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Josiah Rogers Papers. Manuscripts and Archives, Yale University Library.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This collection consists of approximately two hundred items purchased from Maury A. Bromsen in 1959, and covering the period 1692-1764. The papers relate primarily to the estate of Josiah Rogers of Branford, Connecticut. Rogers, who was born in 1679, worked as a blacksmith, miller, and farmer, and acquired a large estate before his death sometime in 1757. Excerpts from a detailed description of the collection sent by the dealer follow:

"These papers were evidently at one time the property of Josiah Rogers...or, if not his papers, they came into the hands of his immediate heirs. They cover the period 1692-1764; the locality is mainly Branford, but there are documents concerned with Milford, Stratford, New London, Killingworth (Clinton), New Haven, and Waterbury. Most of them are cessions of property to Josiah Rogers; others reflect the day-to-day life of the community.... Four of the documents are printed forms, and the types are almost certainly those of Thomas Short of New London, Connecticut's first printer...."

"Among the important signatures present are those of Joseph Talcott, Connecticut's first Connecticut-born governor, and Gurdon Saltonstall, cleric, colonial governor, and a prime mover in the establishment of the Collegiate School which became YaleCollege...."

"John Russell, sixth child of Rev. Samuel Russell (in whose home Yale was founded), is represented by numerous signatures. So is his presumed brother Jonathan Russell. In 1826 John Russell was made clerk of the church society. The commonest signature here is that of Edward Baker, who as justice of the peace officially endorsed many papers...."
The papers are arranged in a single chronological sequence.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: For a chart outlining the genealogical relations of Josiah Rogers, please consult theGenealogical Chart.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by the creator(s) of this collection are in the public domain. There are no restrictions on use. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

geogname.part: United States -- History -- Colonial period, ca. 1600-1775
